title: Matt Kenseth and how he changed NASCAR forever
text:
NASCAR, a sport overshadowed by literally everything, every molecule, everyone and their mother that you can possibly think of. As much as I have been a fan of NASCAR and many drivers apart from it, It’s a very love-hate relationship, From bad organizational management to main streaming gimmicks that ruin the competition, sponsors that shuffle around like a deck of cards, and all of it leading to dozens and dozens and dozens of empty seats week after week, it’s a hard time to be a fan of the sport. It’s even overshadowed by F1 nowadays, a global organization that has a better market value to appeal to people in America than the race organization that was supposed to appeal to Americans. Do you expect me to convince a bunch of 20 year olds that turning left for 4 hours is exciting? No, but I shall try to make it seem less boring than most people say it to be. Now, If you asked me this question about 30 years ago, I’d say I can sell you this as if I were a girl scout selling cookies. Hell, there was once a time where their viewership was almost on par with the NFL. But what happened? How did this sport go from the thing that everyone was talking about to the thing the weird kid in the back of the bus likes? (I am that weird kid) Well lets time travel back to the great age of 2003, the moment where one of the most controversial championship seasons occurred, and it came from the most unlikely of drivers…


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(nascaR.data)
library(gt)

cup <- load_series("cup")

Mk <- get_driver_info("Matt Kenseth", type = "season")

MattKseason <- cup |> 
  filter(Season == 2003) |> 
  group_by(Driver) |> 
  summarise(
    total_Pts= sum(Pts),
    total_Wins= sum(Win)
) |> arrange(desc(total_Pts))  

MattKseason |>
  filter(total_Pts > 3000) |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(total_Pts= "Points", 
            total_Wins= "Race Win") |> 
  tab_header(
    title = "How Matt Kenseths 2003 Championship season changed Nascar",
    subtitle = "This season defined of how consistent top 10 finishes and a well running race car can create a winning season, but that begs the question, is staying consistent more important than winning the race?"
) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("Source: nascaR.data | By: Joshua Machan")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |> 
   data_color(
    columns = vars(total_Pts),
    colors = scales::col_numeric(
      palette = c(
        "white", "orange", "red"),
      domain = NULL)
  ) |> 
   data_color(
    columns = vars(total_Wins),
    colors = scales::col_numeric(
      palette = c(
        "white","green", "blue"),
      domain = NULL)
  )








How Matt Kenseths 2003 Championship season changed Nascar


This season defined of how consistent top 10 finishes and a well running race car can create a winning season, but that begs the question, is staying consistent more important than winning the race?


Driver
Points
Race Win

0



Source: nascaR.data | By: Joshua Machan








The name at the very top of the list may not be one you can easily think of when you think of NASCAR. Not Dale Earnhardt, not Jeff Gordon, but a guy from Cambrdige, Wisconsin named Matt Kenseth. In just his 6th year at the highest level of NASCAR, he won the season championship. Now can you tell me what the issue is with this graph? Anyone? That’s right, there’s only one win. It was the third race of the season and didn’t win a race until the very next year in Rockingham, North Carolina. He’s the only driver to win the championship with one win in the modern era of NASCAR, and the fourth driver to do so in the sports history. While Kenseth did have a single win, his biggest factor of staying on top was his consistency, finishing in the top 10 a total of 25 times out of 36 races. If anyone has a calculator on them, that’s 69% of the 2003 season. Now another thing that’s out of place is the driver who finished in 5th that season, Ryan Newman, another driver who has had a great but interesting career. Don’t you all find it odd that the driver with the most wins of 8, finished in 5th? If I were Ryan, I’d be pissed. Here’s a bigger question though, was this just a one and done season or was Matt actually better than his championship season?


Code
MattBG <-Mk |> 
  filter(Series == "Cup") |> 
  group_by(Season) |> 
  summarise(
    total_Avg_Finish = sum(`Avg Finish`),
    total_Wins= sum(Wins)
) |> arrange(desc(total_Wins)) 

SEVENWIN <- MattBG |> 
  filter(Season == 2013) 
CHAMPION <- MattBG |> 
  filter(Season == 2003)

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=MattBG, aes(x=Season, weight= total_Wins)) +
  geom_bar(data=SEVENWIN, aes(x=Season, weight= total_Wins),fill="yellow") + 
  geom_bar(data=CHAMPION, aes(x=Season, weight= total_Wins),fill="red") +
coord_flip() + 
  labs(
    x="Win Count", 
    title="Matt Kenseths Career: A Wild Millenium", 
    subtitle="Through the ups and downs, Kenseth has had some good strides and some missteps.", 
    caption="Source: nascaR.data | By: Joshua Machan"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Now after that 2003 Season, NASCAR thought “Well we can’t let something like this happen again, We want a champion with wins and consistency, not just consistency” Thus the Matt Kenseth Rule was officially adopted the next year, creating the Chase for the Cup. Looking at Matt’s Career, he had a very up and down career, and yet 2003 wasn’t even his best season. That Yellow line was his first year with his new ride at Joe Gibbs Racing, Yes Coach Joe Gibbs from the washington redskins, that Joe Gibbs. Kenseth went on to win 7 wins and finished second in points for the championship behind Jimmie Johnson. A total of 19 points was spread between the two of them under the modified chase for the cup.
Now lets talk about the Chase, on paper this seems like a good way to determine a champion for the season, but there is one main flaw that debunks the system, that being the unpredictable nature of the sport. Pole leaders change each race, every pit stop matters, debris can come out of nowhere and destroy a car’s chance of winning, and on track rivalries can happen. If there’s one thing that could ruin a championship run, it’s revenge when you least expect it.
Just two years later after his second place finish for the cup, Kenseth gets spun out by Joey Logano in Kansas and dashes his hopes for the championship as he would be eliminated the following week at Talladega after Joey wins his sixth race that season. The next week in Martinsville, he intentionally wrecks him and does the exact same thing that Logano did to him just two weeks prior. One thing you need to know about Matt Kenseth is that he is considered one of the nicest guys in the garage and he’s the least likely to get a temper on the track, so it was very uncharacteristic of him to do this. But when it comes to a guy like Joey Logano, who is by far one of the most controversial drivers in the sports history, I personally don’t blame him. This moment in Martinsville is regarded as one of the most infamous moments in recent memory, and is a perfect example of how the playoff system isn’t so picture perfect. Matt Kenseth’s Teammate Denny Hamlin said after the crash occurred “I just can’t get over what this series has come to. Just how f****** up it is. How do you really crown a real champion now? It’s just the wild, wild west.” This moment became so infamous that NASCAR had to add more to the Matt Kenseth rule. So yeah the Chase is a mixed bag to say the least. Yet with how the points are distributed after multiple playoff and race format changes. Do you think that changes would affect Kenseth and the best drivers?


Code
top20 <- cup |> 
  filter(Season >= 2003) |> 
  group_by(Driver) |> 
  summarize(
    TotalPoints = sum(Pts, na.rm=TRUE)
  ) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  top_n(20, wt=TotalPoints)

FinLinechart <- cup |> 
  filter(Season >= 2003) |> 
  filter(Driver %in% top20$Driver) |> 
  group_by(Season, Driver) |> 
  summarise(
    total_Avg_Finish = mean(Finish),
    total_Wins= sum(Win)
) |> arrange(desc(total_Avg_Finish)) 


ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=FinLinechart, aes(x=Season, y=total_Avg_Finish, group=Driver)) + facet_wrap(~Driver) + 
  labs(
    y="Total Points", 
    title="Nascars Biggest Point Getters", 
    subtitle="These drivers were known as some of the greatest in their era, heres a look at where Matt stands in the mix.", 
    caption="Source: nascaR.data | By: Joshua Machan"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Some of these drivers retired in the mid 2000s and 2010s hence why some of them are often shorter lines than others. You have your best modern drivers like Kyle Busch, Jimmie Johnson, and Martin Truex Jr. just to name a few, and our good ole chaos maker Kenseth sits around 18th within the 20 best point getters in the modern era of NASCAR. With that Matt Kenseth Rule changing the format of how to crown a champion, this awoke something in NASCAR to try to mainstream the sport even more. Well if you know the old saying, if it aint broke then bash with a crow bar and scatter the remains. What do I mean by this? Over the last couple of years, NASCAR has been changing the way they distribute points, and are currently still doing, ugh Stage Points. Ever wanted to see segmented racing in the actual race? me neither it sounds like it will make the playoff picture even worse than when Kenseth won it by just being consistent. I understand their thought process behind this because they wanted to limit the amount of just boredom of watching them go around, but at the same time it leads to massive inconsistency within the top drivers because now it’s essentially bonus points for where they finish one third or two thirds the way into a race. Ok, I’m off my soap box about the stage points.
So where does NASCAR go into the new age? Personally I feel like meddling from the top needs to stop as soon as possible, but at the same time drivers that change up the status quo make it seem more interesting and less robotic if that makes sense. Matt Kenseth always struck me as such an interesting driver. He didn’t have a brash racing style or outspoken personality, but rather he did what nobody else did in NASCAR. He became so consistent that he literally rewrote the game itself. In a sport (YES A SPORT) like NASCAR, It’s about timing, power and the ability to outlast your opponents in the fastest environment in the world, but not many men has done it like Matt Kenseth. So the next time if you’re flipping through channels, and happen to land at cars turning left, take some time to appreciate the art, the intricacies, the craziness, and the sport of NASCAR.

title: The Truest Underdog in the March Madness Tournament
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(ggrepel)

team26 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2026.csv")

team25 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2025.csv")

team24 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2024.csv")

team23 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2023.csv")

team22 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2022.csv")

team21 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2021.csv")

team20 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2020.csv")

team19 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2019.csv")

team18 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2018.csv")

team17 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2017.csv")

team16 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2016.csv")

team15 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2015.csv")

team14 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2014.csv")

team13 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2013.csv")

team12 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2012.csv")

team11 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2011.csv")

team10 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2010.csv")

team09 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2009.csv")

team08 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2008.csv")

team07 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2007.csv")

team06 <-read_csv("ncaa_mens_basketball_team_stats_2006.csv")

teams<- bind_rows(team06,team07,team08,team09,team10,team11,team12,team13,team14,team15,team16,team17,team18,team19,team20,team21,team22,team23,team24,team25,team26) |> 
  mutate(teamseason = paste(school, season))

mostimprobables <- teams |> filter(teamseason %in% c("George Mason 2005-2006", "Virginia Commonwealth 2010-2011", "Florida Gulf Coast 2012-2013", "Loyola (IL) 2017-2018", "Saint Peter's 2021-2022", "NC State 2023-2024", "Davidson 2007-2008", "Oral Roberts 2020-2021", "Florida Atlantic 2022-2023", "UCLA 2020-2021", "Syracuse 2015-2016"))


The greatest moments of sports in my opinion is when David slays Goliath, and nothing is more of a greater example than March Madness. For every year since the days of World War 2, teams from around the nation battled on the hardwood to determine who is the best of the best. Legends were made, dynasties were built, and of course, the unlikely of small schools making an unbelievable deep run in the tournament. There have been only a handful of teams that have been labeled as underdogs or where they never should have gone so far, however the more successful teams have been in the past 20 years. Small schools such as Virginia Commonwealth, Loyola Chicago, and Florida Atlantic, just to name a few, have proven to hung with the big boys, but how improbable are these runs? To answer this, I compared 11 schools that have defied the odds by making a deep run in the tournament. It’s time to see where these teams have stacked up.


Code
ggplot() +
  geom_point(data= teams, aes(x= w_l_percent, y= srs), color = "grey") +   
  geom_point(data= mostimprobables, aes(x= w_l_percent, y= srs), color="red") +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=mostimprobables, 
    aes(x=w_l_percent, y=srs, label=teamseason), size=3) +
  labs(
    x="Win/Loss Percentage", 
    y="Simple Rating System", 
    title="Scheduling and Competition", 
    subtitle="See how percentages of the each teams competion stacks to their wins and losses.", 
    caption="Source: Data Sportsrefrence.com | By: Joshua Machan"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









With comparing their srs and their win loss percentage we can see how great some of the teams were when it came to playing within their conference and outside of their conference, and then we can see the teams that have a lower srs and win loss percentage comparison to the ones that are higher up, meaning that they had consistent success in their games and within their competition. One that sticks out the most is the teams that made it to the sweet 16: Oral Roberts, Florida Gulf Coast, and Saint Peters. They have had an amazing season but were unable to progress forward with other competition which could explain why they are the lowest ones in the scatterplot. It shows that while they didn’t make a far run in the tournament, but it gives us what competition they had to face to get that low of a win-loss percentage than the other teams. The most important thing besides winning games has to be shooting, how will you win the game if you can’t shoot the ball?


Code
mostimprobables |> 
  select(teamseason, fg_percent) |> 
  arrange(desc(fg_percent)) |>
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    fg_percent = "Shooting Percentage"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Do these underdogs stats defy the odds of shooting?",
    subtitle = "These miracoulus runs are awed by many, but does their field goal stat show how unprecident their run truly was?",
    ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Joshua Machan  |  **Source:** Sportsreference.com")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
  ) 








Do these underdogs stats defy the odds of shooting?


These miracoulus runs are awed by many, but does their field goal stat show how unprecident their run truly was?


teamseason
Shooting Percentage




Loyola (IL) 2017-2018
0.507


George Mason 2005-2006
0.481


Davidson 2007-2008
0.469


Florida Atlantic 2022-2023
0.466


Florida Gulf Coast 2012-2013
0.462


UCLA 2020-2021
0.461


NC State 2023-2024
0.450


Oral Roberts 2020-2021
0.449


Virginia Commonwealth 2010-2011
0.434


Saint Peter's 2021-2022
0.427


Syracuse 2015-2016
0.425



By: Joshua Machan | Source: Sportsreference.com








Shooting percentages are also a huge factor for these tournament runs, if you can’t shoot, you can’t score. According to the typical baseline to determine if a team has good shooting percentages, the average is about 45-48%. The worst is around 42% which two teams have that distinct honor, St Peter’s in 2022 and Syracuse in 2016. I believe both of these teams make a strong case for having an unpredictable run with these percentages but also of how they got to the tournament. St Peter’s was your average small school that made the blue bloods look like chumps and made them the second 15th seed to make it to the Sweet 16, but Syracuse in 2016 was in a much different boat. They are a much bigger school but they had an off season that probably should have sent them to the National Invitational Tournament, they were eliminated in the second round of their conference tournament but somehow made it to the final four. This team really makes it a strong case to be the tourist underdog. Coincidentally another ACC team recently had a run like that as well. NC State was the First Four team to ever make it to the Final Four having a similar seed and placement. The only difference was that NC State had a slightly better shooting percentage and had more rebounds than Syracuse. The bigger thing with how shooting is affected when rebounding gives you a second chance of scoring?


Code
mostimprobablesdrb <- mostimprobables |> 
  mutate(drb = trb - orb)

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data=mostimprobablesdrb, 
    aes(
      y= reorder(teamseason, drb), yend= teamseason,
      x= orb, xend= drb)
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=mostimprobablesdrb,
    aes(
      y=teamseason,
      x=orb,
      color="Offensive rebounds"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=mostimprobablesdrb,
    aes(
      y=teamseason,
      x=drb,
      color="Defensive rebounds"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + scale_color_manual(name="Rebounds", values=c("green", "blue")) +
  labs( 
    x= "Rebounds",
    y= "Teams and Season",  
    title = "Rebound and bouncing back.",
    subtitle = "Which team has the rebounds to back up their shooting percentage?", 
    caption="Source: SportsRefrence.com | By: Joshua Machan"
  )+
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Rebounding is a key figure of how to get back in the game. Comparing the lines, oral roberts had the worst offensive rebounds during their season. It makes it a clear comparison that they weren’t going to last long in the tournament with how many second chances they had on offense. NC State and Florida Atlantic were a very heavy rebounding team which is how I think they made a far run in the tournament, but the bigger thing with how some of these teams have less rebounds than others, which makes it why some of these tournament runs seem so improbable. Rebounding is a key figure of how you can get second chance points, and to have so many teams having less rebounds like Virginia Commonwealth in 2011 and UCLA in 2021, It has a greater impact than expected.
Underdogs will never go away, they are what make sports worth watching, the little guy with nothing to lose beats the immortal titan of the gods puts the madness in March Madness. In looking over the data that I have found and made a conclusion on which team in the past 20 years is the truest underdog in the men’s march madness tournament I believe is a tie between Oral Roberts and Syracuse. While Oral Roberts never made it farther in the tournament then others, comparing all three of the graphs, they had some of the worst compared to every single other team, Why is this a factor you may ask? How would any team get far with bad stats, most are first round exits, but they somehow made a Sweet 16 run with a lower srs and win loss percentage, and less rebounds than the other teams listed. Syracuse was also a strong contender considering they have had the worst shooting percentage amongst the teams highlighted. With how they started, they became the most not talked about run in March Madness history. These teams and many others all try to live up to one thing: Winning. It’s what everyone wants to do, but these few are the rarest of rare, they create memories for themselves, their family, and the many many sports fans that love a protagonist
